vocabulario.palabra: cost an arm and a leg
vocabulario.traduccion: costar un ojo de la cara

banco_oraciones.oracion: That new phone cost Ben an arm and a leg.
Sofía's studio is a safe bet — she's never lost a client.
Abuela Carmen says cutting corners always costs more later.
They paid through the nose for that apartment.
Sofía doesn't cut corners, even when a client is in a hurry.
The business didn't go under, despite the hard year.
We didn't make ends meet that first year of the studio.
Did that renovation cost an arm and a leg?
Is it a safe bet to invest in her studio?
How do freelancers make ends meet in a slow month?
Did the business go bust? No, it didn't.
Did you pay through the nose for the flight? Yes, I did.
Is Sofía's studio a safe bet? Yes, it is.

interaccion: Sofía: This new laptop **cost me an arm and a leg**.
Abuela Carmen: Then don't **cut corners** on the case — protect it, or you'll **pay through the nose** fixing it later.
Sofía: You always have a saying for everything.
Abuela Carmen: When you sold mangoes for thirty years, you learn every trick to **make ends meet**.

banner_diagnostico.opcion_a: The phone cost an arm and a leg, so now I only have one arm.
banner_diagnostico.opcion_b: The phone cost an arm and a leg, so I can't afford anything else this month.

inicio.definicion_pragmatica: Idioms about money describe cost, risk and survival using images (arms, legs, corners, noses) that have nothing literally to do with money — the meaning is entirely figurative.

tabla.titulo: Money idioms — literal image vs. real meaning
filas: cost an arm and a leg
taking a shortcut on a shape
do something cheaply/badly to save money or time
pay much more than something is worth
have just enough money to live on

ejercicios.pregunta: That new phone ___ Ben an arm and a leg.
Abuela Carmen says cutting corners always ___ more later.
They ___ through the nose for that apartment.

ejercicios.afirmacion: 'Cost an arm and a leg' means something is very cheap.
'Cut corners' means to do something poorly to save time or money.
'Go bust' means a business becomes very successful.

ejercicios.texto: Sofía doesn't ___ corners, even in a hurry.
The business didn't go ___, despite the hard year.
We didn't ___ ends meet that first year.

ejercicios.respuesta: that new phone cost Ben an arm and a leg
they paid through the nose for that apartment

ejercicios.texto_con_error: The phone cost an arm and a leg, so now I only have one arm.
ejercicios.respuesta: The phone cost an arm and a leg, so I can't afford anything else this month.

ejercicios.instruccion: Rewrite using 'cost an arm and a leg'.
ejercicios.respuesta: The laptop cost an arm and a leg.

ejercicios.oracion_base: Is it a safe bet to invest in her studio?
Sofía cuts corners on every project.
ejercicios.respuesta: Sofía doesn't cut corners on every project.

ejercicios.pregunta: Have you ever paid through the nose for something? (short answer)

guion: Sofía: This new laptop cost me an arm and a leg.
Abuela Carmen: Then don't cut corners on the case — protect it, or you'll pay through the nose fixing it later.
Sofía: You always have a saying for everything.
Abuela Carmen: When you sold mangoes for thirty years, you learn every trick to make ends meet.
Sofía: Did you ever worry the stand would go bust?
Abuela Carmen: Every single week. And it never did.

comprension.pregunta: What does Abuela Carmen warn Sofía about?
opciones: Cutting corners on the laptop case
comprension.respuesta: Cutting corners on the laptop case

comprension.pregunta: Did Abuela Carmen's mango stand ever go bust?

reading.texto: Abuela Carmen sold mangoes on the same corner for thirty years, and she says every idiom about money in English has a mango-stand equivalent in her own history. When Sofía complains that a new laptop cost her an arm and a leg, Abuela Carmen nods — she remembers years when a single bad harvest meant paying through the nose for fruit she used to grow herself. When people ask if her business ever nearly went bust, she just laughs: 'Every week felt like it might. And every week, somehow, we made ends meet.' Her one rule, repeated to Sofía more times than either can count: never cut corners on the thing your reputation depends on.

preguntas.afirmacion: Abuela Carmen sold mangoes for thirty years.
She says her business never felt like it might fail.
Her business actually went bust at some point.
Her rule is to never cut corners on her reputation.
